title: Strong core and Pareto-optimality in the multiple partners matching problem under lexicographic preference domains
abstract: We study strong core and Pareto-optimal solutions for multiple partners matching problem under lexicographic preference domains from a computational point of view. The restriction to the two-sided case is called stable many-to-many matching problem and the general one-sided case is called stable fixtures problem. We provide an example to show that the strong core can be empty even for many-to-many problems, and that deciding the non-emptiness of the strong core is NP-hard. On the positive side, we give efficient algorithms for finding a near feasible strong core solution and for finding a fractional matching in the strong core of fractional matchings. In contrast with the NP-hardness result for the stable fixtures problem, we show that finding a maximum size matching that is Pareto-optimal can be done efficiently for many-to-many problems. Finally, we show that for reverse-lexicographic preferences the strong core is always non-empty in the many-to-many case.
summary_en: Matching problems with multiple partners (like hospital-doctor networks with multiple positions, school-student assignments with multi-year attendance) can have complex core structures. This paper studies computational tractability of strong core and Pareto-optimal solutions under lexicographic preferences. Findings: the strong core can be empty even for many-to-many problems; deciding non-emptiness is NP-hard. On the positive side, efficient algorithms exist for finding near-feasible strong-core solutions and fractional matchings. The paper tightens the computational characterization of a classical matching problem, relevant to market-design practice.
data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Computational-complexity analysis.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian market-design applications: kidney-exchange programmes (with multi-donor options), teacher-school matching, university admissions with multiple offers. The paper informs practical algorithm choice when designing real allocation mechanisms. Relevant to the Péter Bíró-led KRTK game-theory group's ongoing work on kidney exchange. Caveat: hardness is worst-case; realistic instances often admit fast solutions.
